regions: Frontier posts: Great Plains, Southwest, Pacific Northwest (1790s-1890s)
Coastal fortifications: Atlantic and Gulf coasts
Overseas garrisons: Philippines (1898-1946), Panama Canal Zone, Hawaii, Germany (post-1945)
Stateside installations: Fort Leavenworth, Fort Riley, Fort Benning, Fort Bragg, Presidio

what_they_did.daily_work: The peacetime regular army was defined by routine. A typical garrison day began with reveille and roll call, followed by drill (infantry, cavalry, or artillery), fatigue duty (construction, road-building, maintenance, stable care), guard duty, and inspections. Frontier soldiers patrolled territory, escorted supply trains, built and maintained posts, and engaged in intermittent combat during the Indian Wars. Between wars, the army was small, isolated, and consumed by bureaucratic routine. Career soldiers served through cycles of peacetime boredom and wartime mobilization — Indian Wars, Spanish-American War, Philippine Insurrection, Punitive Expedition into Mexico, WWI, interwar garrison duty, WWII, Korea.
what_they_did.seasonal_cycle: Frontier operations followed seasonal patterns — campaigns were typically mounted in spring through fall, with winter spent in garrison. Post-frontier, the army's cycle revolved around training schedules, annual maneuvers, and inspection cycles rather than seasons.
tools_and_equipment: Pack and garrison equipment (shelter half, mess kit, canteen)
Field artillery pieces (various calibers by era)
what_they_did.typical_hours: Garrison duty followed a fixed daily schedule from reveille (typically 5:30-6:00 AM) to taps (9:00-10:00 PM). Actual duties occupied 6-8 hours, but soldiers were subject to call at all times. In the field or in combat, there were no fixed hours.

economics.typical_arrangement: Enlisted soldiers received monthly pay supplemented by rations, quarters (or a housing allowance), clothing allowances, and access to the post exchange (PX). Pay increased with rank and longevity. Officers received substantially higher pay plus additional allowances. Retirement pay (after 30 years of service) was available from 1885 onward.
economics.annual_income_range: Private's pay: $13/month (1870s-1900s), $15/month (1908), $30/month (1917 WWI), $50/month (1942 WWII base). Corporal: $15-$18/month (1870s-1900s). Sergeant: $22-$34/month (1870s-1900s). First Sergeant: $30-$75/month (by 1900s). Career NCOs with 20+ years of service and senior rank could earn $75-$150/month by WWI — modest but stable income with all basic needs provided.
economics.comparison: A private in the 1890s earning $13/month ($156/year) earned less than almost any civilian laborer — but received food, shelter, clothing, and medical care. For immigrants with no English, men with no trade, and those with no other options, the total compensation package made enlistment rational. Career NCOs eventually earned a living comparable to a skilled tradesman, with the added security of guaranteed employment and a pension.
economics.debt_cycle: Soldiers were not typically caught in debt cycles, but gambling and alcohol consumption at frontier posts could leave men perpetually broke. Paydays were monthly, and the gap between paydays was bridged by credit at sutler's stores (post traders) and later at the post exchange.

hazards: Disease — the leading killer in every American war through 1917 (malaria, typhoid, dysentery, yellow fever)
Desertion consequences — flogging (abolished 1861), branding, hard labor, dishonorable discharge
Alcoholism, widely prevalent in isolated frontier garrisons
Injuries from horse handling, construction labor, and weapons accidents
working_conditions.living_conditions: Frontier posts were often crude — barracks were drafty wooden or adobe structures, sanitation was poor, food was monotonous (salt pork, hardtack, beans, coffee). Post-frontier garrison life improved gradually but remained austere. Married enlisted men were discouraged from bringing families — 'on the economy' housing near posts was often substandard. Officers' Row was a different world — larger quarters, servants (often enlisted men detailed as orderlies), and social life modeled on genteel civilian patterns.
working_conditions.family_involvement: Officers' wives managed households that functioned as social and diplomatic units on post. Enlisted men's families, when present, occupied 'Soapsuds Row' — laundresses' quarters — where wives earned income doing laundry for the garrison. Children grew up on military posts and often enlisted themselves. The 'army brat' identity is generations deep in some families.
working_conditions.autonomy: Enlisted soldiers had minimal autonomy — military discipline governed every aspect of daily life. NCOs had somewhat more discretion in executing orders. Officers operated within the chain of command but enjoyed considerable personal freedom off duty. The paradox of career military service was total institutional control combined with geographic adventure and guaranteed employment.

push_factors: Completion of enlistment term (3-5 years, depending on era)
Physical disability from wounds or disease
Desertion — rates were staggeringly high (33% in some years during the 1870s-1890s)
Reduction in force after major wars (post-Civil War, post-WWI, post-WWII)
why_they_left.what_replaced_them: Career soldiers were not replaced by technology — the Regular Army evolved into the modern professional military. The transition from frontier constabulary to industrial-age fighting force (1890s-1940s) and then to Cold War global garrison (post-1945) transformed the occupation without eliminating it. What changed was scale: the peacetime army grew from 25,000 in 1890 to over 1.5 million by 1955.
why_they_left.narrative_hook: If {ancestor_name} served as a regular in {county} around {year}, the service records at the National Archives may contain more biographical detail than any other single source — physical description, medical history, conduct record, and every post where the soldier served.

records_generated.record_type: Military service records (compiled service records and enlistment papers)
records_generated.what_it_shows: Name, age, birthplace, physical description, enlistment date and location, regiment and company, promotions, transfers, medical treatment, disciplinary actions, discharge date and type
records_generated.where_to_find: NARA in Washington, DC (pre-1912 records) and National Personnel Records Center, St. Louis (post-1912). Pre-Civil War and Civil War records are available on fold3.com and FamilySearch.
records_generated.research_tip: Military service records are among the most detailed biographical documents in American genealogy. A single file can span decades and include physical descriptions, medical histories, and conduct records. The 1973 fire at NPRC destroyed many 20th-century Army records — always request what survives.

records_generated.what_it_shows: Applicant's service history, medical conditions, family members (wife, children, dependents), supporting affidavits from fellow soldiers, financial circumstances
records_generated.where_to_find: NARA (Record Group 15 — Veterans Administration records). Pension index available on Ancestry.com and FamilySearch.
records_generated.research_tip: Pension files are genealogical treasure. Applications required proof of service, identity, and family relationships. A widow's pension application might include the marriage certificate, children's birth dates, and sworn statements from neighbors — an entire family history in one file.

records_generated.what_it_shows: Monthly listing of all personnel present at a military post or in a company, with status (present, absent, sick, on detached service, deserted)
records_generated.where_to_find: NARA (Record Group 94 — Adjutant General's Office). Available on microfilm and increasingly digitized.
records_generated.research_tip: Post returns allow you to track a soldier's movements month by month across his entire career. They show when a soldier arrived at a post, when he left, and why. Combined with the post's location, they create a geographic biography.

records_generated.what_it_shows: Charges, testimony, verdict, sentence — detailed narrative accounts of incidents
records_generated.where_to_find: NARA (Record Group 153 — Judge Advocate General). Major cases are indexed; minor cases require knowing the soldier's unit and approximate date.
records_generated.research_tip: Courts-martial records contain sworn testimony that provides vivid, detailed accounts of daily military life — even minor cases (drunkenness, absence without leave, insubordination) reveal the texture of garrison life that no other record captures.

census_clues.what_to_look_for: Soldiers enumerated at military posts will appear on census pages listing the entire garrison. Look for birthplace and age to identify your ancestor among many soldiers. A man listed as a 'soldier' in multiple censuses spanning 10-20 years is a career regular, not a wartime volunteer. Buffalo Soldier regiments (9th/10th Cavalry, 24th/25th Infantry) can be identified by race and unit designation.
census_clues.red_flags: An ancestor who appears as a 'soldier' at a frontier post in one census and as a 'laborer' in a Western town in the next may have deserted or completed his enlistment and stayed in the region. Desertion was so common that its absence from a record is as noteworthy as its presence.

cultural_context: The Regular Army in peacetime was a marginal institution in American life. Citizens distrusted standing armies, and soldiers were often treated as social outcasts — refused service in saloons, barred from respectable boarding houses, and looked down upon by the civilians they protected. The phrase 'gone for a soldier' carried connotations of failure or desperation. For Black men, however, the Regular Army offered something available almost nowhere else in post-Civil War America: steady pay, institutional respect within the military, and a measure of dignity. The Buffalo Soldiers of the 9th and 10th Cavalry and 24th and 25th Infantry compiled distinguished service records while enduring the same racial hostility from civilians and white soldiers alike.

counter_narratives.claim: Career soldiers were not always motivated by patriotism
counter_narratives.context: The peacetime Regular Army drew heavily from men with limited alternatives — recent immigrants with no English, unemployed laborers, men fleeing debts or legal trouble, and those with no family or community ties. In the 1880s-1890s, roughly one-third to one-half of enlisted men were foreign-born. Irish and German immigrants were the largest groups, followed by English, Scandinavians, and others. The army was, in part, an immigrant assimilation institution.

counter_narratives.claim: Frontier army life was monotonous, not romantic
counter_narratives.context: The popular image of cavalry charges and frontier heroism masks the reality that most frontier soldiers spent the vast majority of their time on fatigue duty — digging ditches, building roads, hauling supplies, and maintaining posts. Actual combat was infrequent, brief, and terrifying. The leading causes of death were disease, accident, and suicide, not enemy action. Desertion rates of 25-33% reflect the gap between recruitment promises and daily reality.

counter_narratives.claim: The Buffalo Soldiers' story is not a simple narrative of military glory
counter_narratives.context: The Black regular regiments served with distinction by any measure, but their service included enforcing policies of Indian removal and suppression that inflicted tremendous harm on Native peoples. They also faced relentless racism from white officers, soldiers, and the communities near their posts. Their history demands recognition of both their achievement and the moral complexity of the missions they were ordered to carry out.

source_references.author: Coffman, Edward M.
source_references.title: The Old Army: A Portrait of the American Army in Peacetime, 1784-1898
source_references.notes: Definitive study of the peacetime Regular Army with extensive treatment of enlisted life, desertion, and the frontier garrison system

source_references.author: Leckie, William H.
source_references.title: The Buffalo Soldiers: A Narrative of the Negro Cavalry in the West
source_references.notes: Pioneering history of the Black regular regiments on the frontier, essential for any narrative involving African American career soldiers

source_references.author: Wooster, Robert
source_references.title: The Military and United States Indian Policy, 1865-1903
source_references.notes: Examines the Regular Army's role in Indian policy with attention to garrison life and the career soldiers who carried out these policies

source_references.author: Foner, Jack D.
source_references.title: The United States Soldier Between Two Wars: Army Life and Reforms, 1865-1898
source_references.notes: Detailed study of enlisted life in the post-Civil War Regular Army including pay, conditions, desertion, and reform efforts
